Notice Of Motion To Compel Contractual Arbitration And Motion To Stay Action [Code Civ. Proc., 1281, 1281.2, 1281.4]
Off Calendar Jun-17-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Katerina U, Esq. for Cross-complainant, Bay Mountain Air, Inc.
Michael T. Joyce, Esq. (510.465.7100; mtj@mcinerney-dillon.com) of McInerney & Dillon for Cross-Defendant, Thompson Builders Corporation.

ARGUED The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT THOMPSON BUILDERS CORPORATION's Motion To Compel Contractual Arbitration And Motion To Stay Action [Code Civ. Proc., 1281, 1281.2, 1281.4]:

THOMPSON BUILDERS CORPORATION's ("TBC") motion to compel arbitration and stay is denied.

First, TBC fails to show that the DRB process outlined in the parties' agreement is tantamount to an agreement to arbitrate post-project disputes even though the process is described as "binding." The DRB procedure is usually non-binding and meant to resolve disputes during the course of construction to allow the construction process to move forward without litigation. (See Sehulster Tunnels/Pre-Con v. Traylor Brothers, Inc./Obayashi Corp. (2003) 111 Cal.App.4th 1328, 1338-1339 [explaining the difference between arbitration and the DRB process and noting that the DRB procedure is typically used "in tunneling and other large, complex, heavy construction projects"]; Los Angles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority v. Shea-Kiewit-Kenny (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 676, 683 ["The DRB members are facilitators or mediators, not adjudicators or arbitrators"].)

The court notes that the parties' agreement states that "[t]he DRB Ruling will be admissible in any future legal proceedings" and expressly refers to arbitration/litigation in other sections of the agreement. (Joyce Decl., Exhibit A, at page 8, pars. S.2 , S.3.) The parties' reference to future legal proceedings and their failure to use the term "binding arbitration" in describing the DRB process indicate that they did not intend the DRB process to resolve the instant dispute.

Second, the court denies arbitration because there is a risk of inconsistent rulings. (See Code of Civil Procedure sec. 1281.2(c).) The court can deny arbitration where (1) a party to the arbitration agreement is also a party to a pending court action or special proceeding with a third party; (2) the third-party action arises out of the same transaction or series of related transactions; and (3) there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact. (See Acquire II, Ltd. v. Colton Real Estate Group (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 959, 967-968.) Here, those facts are present. BAY MOUNTAIN AIR, INC. ("BMA") is a party to the alleged arbitration agreement and is still litigating with parties that are not obligated to arbitrate. All of the claims arise out of the same transaction and there is a possibility of inconsistent rulings. For example, one tribunal could find that BMA is obligated to pay plaintiff while another could conclude that TBC should make the payment.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/SRB)
